coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free dual amplification biosensor based on functional nucleic acid and CDs/CoOOH for detection of leukemia fusion gene
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Chenlan
Zhong, Liang
Wan, Peng
Jia, Hengke
Liu, Beizhong
coredata.dc:description: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) is an acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with a specific fusion gene target, PML/RARα fusion gene (PML/RARα), which is formed by the translocation of chromosomes 15 and 17. Detection of PML/RARα is the most reliable parameter for the diagnosis, treatment adjustment, efficacy evaluation, prognosis analysis and relapse prediction of APL. In this study, a novel biosensor was constructed for rapid enzyme-free detection of PML/RARα using DNAzyme and carbon dots/cobalt oxhydroxide nanosheet complexs (CDs/CoOOH). In the detection system, the separated DNAzymes could specifically recognize and bind together by the PML/RARα to form a complete DNAzyme for shearing hairpin probe (HP), then generated trigger, which was the first signal amplification. Then, trigger could hybridize with the capture probe (CP) anchored to streptavidin (SA) modified microplate as well as fluorescence quenching signal probe (SP@CDs/CoOOH). Finally, ascorbic acid (AA) was added to decompose CoOOH and the fluorescence of CDs was released, which was the second signal amplification. Through the dual signal amplification of DNAzyme and CDs/CoOOH, PML/RARα could be detected quickly and sensitively, which overcame the limitation of protein enzyme in traditional fluorescence methods, showing potential clinical application value in the diagnosis and treatment of leukemia.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Functional nucleic acid
Nanomaterials
Fusion gene
Enzyme-free biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of signal probe-modified carbon dots (SP@CDs)
Verification of microplate coated with SA
Applicability of the developed biosensor for PML/RARα fuse gene detection in human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (A) CDs, (B) CoOOH and (C) CDs/CoOOH. (D) Color development of microplate uncoated and coated with SA: (a) microplate filled with H2O; (b) microplate filled with H2O2; (c) microplate coated with SA + H2O, (d) microplate coated with SA + H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Signal characterization of CDs, CoOOH and CDs/CoOOH. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CoCl2 and CoOOH. (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH. (C) Fluorescence signals of CDs (a), CDs/CoOOH (b) and CDs/CoOOH with gradually increasing amounts of AA (c–g). (D) Photo of CoOOH (a), CDs (b), CDs/CoOOH (c) and CDs/CoOOH + AA (d) under UV light.

The results of feasibility verification. (A) Results of PAGE analysis of the first signal amplification: (1) PML/RARα, (2) PML/RARα + DNAzyme A + DNAzymeB, (3) DNAzyme A + DNAzyme B + HP, (4) PML/RARα+ DNAzyme A + DNAzyme B + HP, (5) Trigger, (6) Trigger + CP, (7) Trigger + CP + SP, (8) DNAzyme A + DNAzyme B + HP + CP + SP, and (9) DNAzyme A + DNAzyme B + PML/RARα + HP + CP + SP. (B) Fluorescence of each group in feasibility verification.

The results of experimental conditions optimization. (A) The concentration of HP. (B) The dosage of SP. (C) The ratio of CDs to CoOOH. (D) The dosage of AA. (E) The incubation time for DNAzyme and HP. (F) The incubation time for trigger and SP@CDs/CoOOH.

(A) Calibration curve of fluorescence signal and PML/RARα concentration. (B) Fluorescence signal in response to different concentrations of PML/RARα. (C) Fluorescence signal in response to different interfering genes (Blank; a: NC; b: PML gene; c: RARα gene; d: PML/RARα (S); e: target PML/RARα). The standard deviation of three independent experiments resulted in the error bars.
(A) Calibration curve of fluorescence signal and PML/RARα concentration in serum. (B) Fluorescence signal in response to different concentrations of PML/RARα in serum.

Schematic diagram of enzyme-free dual amplification biosensor based on functional nucleic acid and CDs/CoOOH for detection of leukemia fusion gene. (A) The preparation of SP@CDs/CoOOH, the quenching of CDs fluorescence by CoOOH, and recovery of CDs fluorescence through reduction of CoOOH by AA. (B) The process of DNAzyme recognizing and binding PML/RARα, DNAzyme shearing HP, and the fixation of SP@CDs/CoOOH to the microplate by triggers. (C) The detection of fluorescence through reduction of CoOOH by AA.
Components added to each group in the feasibility verification by fluorescence.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free dual amplification biosensor based on functional nucleic acid and CDs/CoOOH for detection of leukemia fusion gene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Med+X Center for Manufacturing, West China Precision Medicine Industrial Technology Institute, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China Precision Medicine Industrial Technology Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Med+X Center for Manufacturing, West China Precision Medicine Industrial Technology Institute, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610041, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Laboratory of Yongchuan Hospital, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 402160, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Central Laboratory of Yongchuan Hospital, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 402160, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education), College of Laboratory Medicine, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 400016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education), College of Laboratory Medicine, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Central Laboratory of Yongchuan Hospital, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 402160, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) is an acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with a specific fusion gene target, PML/RARα fusion gene (PML/RARα), which is formed by the translocation of chromosomes 15 and 17. Detection of PML/RARα is the most reliable parameter for the diagnosis, treatment adjustment, efficacy evaluation, prognosis analysis and relapse prediction of APL. In this study, a novel biosensor was constructed for rapid enzyme-free detection of PML/RARα using DNAzyme and carbon dots/cobalt oxhydroxide nanosheet complexs (CDs/CoOOH). In the detection system, the separated DNAzymes could specifically recognize and bind together by the PML/RARα to form a complete DNAzyme for shearing hairpin probe (HP), then generated trigger, which was the first signal amplification. Then, trigger could hybridize with the capture probe (CP) anchored to streptavidin (SA) modified microplate as well as fluorescence quenching signal probe (SP@CDs/CoOOH). Finally, ascorbic acid (AA) was added to decompose CoOOH and the fluorescence of CDs was released, which was the second signal amplification. Through the dual signal amplification of DNAzyme and CDs/CoOOH, PML/RARα could be detected quickly and sensitively, which overcame the limitation of protein enzyme in traditional fluorescence methods, showing potential clinical application value in the diagnosis and treatment of leukemia.

fluorescent biosensing strategy based on enzyme-free dual amplification for the detection of PML-RARα fusion gene.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is an aggressive blood and bone marrow cancer, which is grievous for complications of fever, infection, hemorrhage, and anemia [1,2]. Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), as a subtype of AML, is considered as one of the most rapidly lethal forms of AML with an early death occurrence related to hemorrhage of 26% [3]. Transcriptional deregulation plays an important role in leukemia, and the oncogenic fusion protein PML/RARα, generated by the chromosome translocation t (15; 17), functions as a transcriptional repressor in APL [4]. Meanwhile, the oncogenic fusion protein can cause a differentiation blockade at the promyelocytic differentiation stage, leading to disseminated intravascular coagulation, which is life-threatening [5,6]. PML/RARα, as the molecular target of APL, is important in the diagnosis, treatment monitoring, as well as minimal residual disease (MRD) monitoring [7]. In the clinical laboratory, reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) are usually performed to detect PML/RARα. However, the operation process of FISH is complicated, and this method can miss cryptic translocations, leading to false-negative results [8]. Besides, PCR strategies require expensive instruments, complicated sample pretreatment procedures as well as skilled technicians. Therefore, the establishment of a rapid enzyme-free detection platform has good application prospects for the detection of PML/RARα.

The functional nucleic acids (FNAs), such as aptamers, DNAzymes, ribozymes, and riboswitches, are widely used in enzyme-free amplification strategies [9,10]. Compared with enzyme-depended methods, FNAs possess multiple advantages, such as tailorability, programmability, and specificity in molecular recognition, and can even be further functionalized with optical labels, electrochemical indicators or even nanomaterials, showing promising properties in biomedical applications [11,12]. Furthermore, DNAzyme is a DNA oligonucleotide with high catalytic capacity and can also be cleaved into two inactive fragments (DNAzyme A and DNAzyme B), which can recombine in the presence of targets, demonstrating excellent recognition and specificity [13–15]. Moreover, sequence-specific cleavage and ligation of DNAzyme contribute to the signal amplification element in the biosensing fields [16,17].

Quantum dots (QDs) are nanoscale semiconducting materials which are widely used as fluorescent probes since their excellent optical properties [18,19]. Recently, QDs, including carbon quantum dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs), have become a focal point and are widely used in nanomedicine biosensors development due to the characteristics of high water‐solubility, low toxicity, and unique fluorescent properties [20–24]. However, direct application of QDs may lead to a low signal-to-noise ratio, which affects the detection sensitivity. Luckily, the adoption of quenching materials can immobilize the signal labels and reduce the background signal, then the signal labels are released through the target recognition and fluorescence is recovered, leading significant increasing of signal-to-noise ratio [25–28]. Among a series of quenching materials, cobalt hydroxide (CoOOH) with a simple synthesis step, has a broad and strong absorption spectrum, which can quench the fluorescence of CDs by surface energy transfer (SET). More importantly, CoOOH can be decomposed by ascorbic acid (AA) to eliminate the quenching effect, restoring fluorescence. Based on this feature, fluorescent probe in the form of “on-off-on” can be designed to increase signal-to-noise ratio, increasing the sensitivity of detection. Besides, CoOOH-based fluorescence strategies have been employed in the monitoring biomarkers as well as imaging AA, but the exploration of CoOOH for bio/chemical assay is still at early stage [29–32].

In this work, a novel biosensor was constructed for rapid enzyme-free detection of PML/RARα using DNAzyme and CDs/CoOOH. On one hand, DNAzyme was used to achieve specific recognition of target and effectively shearing of HP, realizing the first signal amplification. On the other hand, the product was converted into fluorescence signal by CDs/CoOOH, and the second signal amplification was achieved. In this way, PML/RARα could be detected quickly and sensitively by the dual signal amplification, overcoming the limitation of protein enzyme activity that was easily affected in traditional fluorescence methods and showing the potential of sensitive detection of PML/RARα in clinical diagnosis.

Sodium oxyhydroxide (NaOH), citric acid (CA) and all purified oligo nucleic acid sequences, including PML/RARα, signal probe (SP), hairpin probe (HP), capture probe (CP), DNAzyme A and B et al. (as shown in Table S1) were obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Biotin (Bio) was labeled at the 3′ end of CP and carboxyl group was modified at the 5′ end of SP. Cobaltous chlorid (CoCl2), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimidehydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, USA). Chron chemicals Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China) provided Sodium hypochlorite (NaClO). Huada Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China) provided streptavidin (SA) and ascorbic acid (AA). Polyethyleneimine (PEI) was obtained from Adamas-beta reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DNAzyme buffer (40 mM Tris, 0.5 mM NaCl, 10 mM KCl and 50 mM MgCl2), 1 × PBS buffer (2 mM KH2PO4, 2.7 mM KCl, 10 mM Na2HPO4, 137 mM NaCl, PH 7.4) and sodium carbonate buffer (12.5 mM NaHCO3, 87.5 mM Na2CO3, pH 9.6) were made of ultra-pure water, which was purified with a Millipore Milli-Q system (Milford, USA). All the reagents were of analytical grade.

Tecnai G2 F20 was provided by FEI Company (Hillsboro, USA). Nanomaterials (e.g., CoOOH, CDs and CDs/CoOOH) were characterized by the High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) provided by FEI Company (Hillsboro, USA) and UV-2550 spectrophotometer provided by Shimadzu company (Kyoto, Japan). Oligonucleotide sequences and product were certified by the electrophoresis apparatus (DYY-6C) provided by the Beijing Liuyi Instrument Factory (Beijing, China) and Gel imager (ChemiDoc XRS) provided by Bio-Rad Laboratories (Hercules, USA). Fluorescence signal was monitored by Synergy™ H1 Hybrid multimode microplate reader provided by BioTek company (Winooski, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of signal probe-modified carbon dots (SP@CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 60 μL EDC (50 mM) and 20 μL SP (2.5 μM) were dispersed into 450 μL MES buffer, and then the mixture was stirred to activate SP. After the addition of 60 μL 50 mM NHS and stirring for 30 min, 10 μL 10 mg/mL CDs were added and stirred for 48 h. Finally, CDs were dialyzed with 1 × PBS buffer, and excess SP was washed off. The capacity was expanded to 400 μL with 1 × PBS buffer, and the obtained SP@CDs were placed at 4 °C in dark for later use.

1.0 mL CoOOH (6.5 mg/mL) and 200 μL SP@CDs were mixed into 1.8 mL deionized water, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min and centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min. Then, the precipitate was obtained as SP@CDs/CoOOH and stored in dark at 4 °C.

20 nM DNAzyme A, 20 nM DNAzyme B, 200 nM HP and samples containing different concentrations of PML-RARα were mixed in DNAzyme buffer and incubated at 37 °C for 90 min. After reaction, 10 μL product and 30 μL SP@CDs/CoOOH were added into the modified microplate, then the microplate was placed in an incubator at 37 °C for 1 h. Afterwards, the supernatant was removed and the microplate was washed 3 times with 1 × PBS buffer. Finally, 200 μL 1 × PBS buffer with 2 μM AA was added and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. Finally, the fluorescence was detected at the maximum emission wavelength (350 nm).

The fluorescence detection platform was based on the dual signal amplification strategy of DNAzyme and CDs/CoOOH. As shown in Scheme 1 A, SP@CDs were assembled on CoOOH to obtain the fluorescence quenching SP@CDs/CoOOH, which would be reduced after the addition of AA, and the fluorescence of CDs would be restored. As shown in Scheme 1B, when PML/RARα existed in the detection system, DNAzyme A and DNAzyme B could combine with it to form a complete DNAzyme to shear HP, generating trigger. Then, one PML/RARα could generate multiple triggers as DNAzyme could continue to shear HP, thereby realizing signal amplification. Moreover, trigger hybridized with SP and CP simultaneously to anchore SP@CDs/CoOOH on the microplate. As shown in Scheme 1C, when AA added in, CoOOH was decomposed and CDs were released, causing the fluorescence signal of CDs to recover, and the fluorescence could be detected, thereby realizing the sensitive detection of PML/RARα.

HRTEM images were used to show the structure and morphology of CDs and CoOOH. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the prepared CDs were homogeneous and monodispersed with an average diameter of around 3 nm (red arrows). Besides, as shown in Fig. 1B, the synthesized CoOOH nanosheet was hexagonal and much larger than CDs, which could provide a large area for CDs immobilization. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1C, a large number of CDs were uniformly distributed on CoOOH.

Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were adopted to explore the optical properties of CDs and CoOOH. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CoCl2 and CoOOH were shown in Fig. 2 A, it could be observed that CoCl2 had a characteristic absorption peak at 510 nm, and CoOOH had characteristic absorption peaks at near 256 nm and 401 nm. With the synthesis of CoOOH, the absorption peak of CoCl2 itself disappeared and was replaced by the broad absorption peak of CoOOH, indicating the successful synthesis of CoOOH. Besides, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH were shown in Fig. 2B, and CDs had an obvious emission peak at 350 nm and an excitation peak at 450 nm, both of which were covered by the absorption peak of CoOOH, indicating that CoOOH could quench the fluorescence of CDs through SET.

Moreover, photo of CoOOH, CDs, CDs/CoOOH and CDs/CoOOH + AA were taken under UV light. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence of CDs (column a) was almost quenched after combining with CoOOH (column b), and the fluorescence was only 1.31% of the previous one. Furthermore, the fluorescence was enhanced with the addition of AA (column c to g), and the CDs fluorescence could be restored to 90.15% of the original one (column g) finally. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2D, there was no fluorescence in CoOOH (a) and CDs/CoOOH (c), while bright fluorescence was observed in CDs (b), indicating that CoOOH could quench the fluorescence of CDs. However, fluorescence could be observed when AA was added to CDs/CoOOH (d), indicating that AA could eliminate the quenching effect of CoOOH and restore fluorescence of CDs. All above indicated that the fluorescence of CDs could be quenched by CoOOH, while AA could eliminate the quenching effect and restore the fluorescence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of microplate coated with SA
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the successful coating of SA on the microplate, the microplate was labeled with Tetramethyl Benzidine (TMB)-labeled SA (TMB-SA) as described above, and H2O2 was added for color development. As shown in Fig. 1D, H2O (a) and H2O2 (b) were added to the wells uncoated with TMB-SA, respectively, and no color revealed, indicating that neither H2O nor H2O2 would react with the microplate uncoated with TMB-SA for color development. However, when H2O (c) and H2O2 (d) were added to the wells coated with TMB-SA, the former showed no color, while the latter appeared blue, indicating that H2O could not react with TMB to develop color, and the blue color only came from the reaction between the TMB and H2O2, which proved that the microplate was successfully coated with SA.

In order to verify whether the developed biosensor could detect the target PML/RARα, PAGE electrophoresis was carried out to verify the shearing part of DNAzyme and the whole experiment. As shown in Fig. 3 A, lane 1 represented PML/RARα, and when DNAzyme A (35 nt) and DNAzyme B (35 nt) were added, a new band (about 110 nt) appeared in lane 2, representing the hybrid product of PML/RARα, DNAzyme A and DNAzyme B. Then, it could be observed in lane 3 that HP (42 nt) would not be sheared when only DNAzyme A, DNAzyme B and HP were present in the reaction system, while a band in lane 4 with the same molecular weight as the synthetic trigger in lane 5 (trigger) was produced with the addition of PML/RARα, indicating that PML/RARα could combine with DNAzyme A and DNAzyme B into a complete DNAzyme, which could shear HP and generate trigger. In addition, a new band (60 nt) was formed in lane 6 as CP (28 nt) was added to the synthetic trigger, indicating that trigger could hybridize with CP. Moreover, a new band with a larger molecular weight than the trigger-CP hybrid band was also formed in lane 7 as both CP and SP were added, which proved that trigger could hybridize with CP and SP simultaneously to form a triplet structure. Meanwhile, the reaction system of DNAzyme A, DNAzyme B, HP, CP and SP was shown in lane 8, which was the negative control group of this protocol. However, the trigger-CP-SP hybrid was only found in lane 9 with the positive control group of PML/RARα, DNAzyme A, DNAzyme B, HP, CP and SP in the reaction system. These results indicated that only in the presence of target PML/RARα, complete DNAzyme would shear HP to generate trigger, and then trigger could hybridize with CP and SP to form the trigger-CP-SP triplet structure.

It was verified by electrophoresis that the shearing part of DNAzyme was effective, and then the whole experiment would be verified through the fluorescence platform (Components added to each group were showed in Table 1 ). In detail, no DNAzyme or any target was added into control column a, and after the first incubation, SP@CDs/CoOOH was added for co-incubation, then the fluorescence was detected as AA was added for incubation at 37 °C for 10 min. In addition, in column b, DNAzyme and HP were added, and after the first incubation, SP@CDs/CoOOH was added for co-incubation, then the fluorescence was detected as AA was added for incubation at 37 °C for 10 min. Besides, in column c, DNAzyme, HP and PML/RARα were added, and after the first incubation, SP@CDs was added for incubation, and the fluorescence was detected directly without adding AA. Meanwhile, column d was similar to column c, while SP@CDs was replaced by SP@CDs/CoOOH. Moreover, experimental column e was added with target PML/RARα according to the routine procedure. As shown in Fig. 3B, almost no fluorescence was shown in column a and b, indicating that DNAzyme A, DNAzyme B and HP had almost no ability to hybridize with CP and SP to produce fluorescence, and the final fluorescence signal output was from the non-specific reaction between residual DNAzyme and HP. Besides, column c and d presented weak fluorescence as well. In addition, the fluorescence of column d was lower than that of column c due to the quenching effect of CoOOH, and the fluorescence of column c was lower than that of experimental column e, indicating that CoOOH could carry multiple CDs, and the final fluorescence signal was more efficient. Moreover, the fluorescence of column d was lower than that of experimental column e, indicating that only when SP was captured on the microplate and the CoOOH was dissolved by AA, CDs could be released to generate obvious fluorescence signal. These results indicated that the designed assay system could realize the detection of the target PML/RARα.
In order to determine the optimal reaction conditions, the PML/RARα of 107 fM was used as the detection sample from the following six aspects.

At first, DNAzyme could shear HP to form multiple triggers, which was the basis of the first signal amplification. Therefore, it was important to explore the optimal HP concentration. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence increased with the increase concentration of HP from 125 nM to 1000 nM and reached the maximum at 1000 nM. Thus, 1000 nM was adopted as the optimal concentration of HP.

Then, SP and CDs were connected by covalent bonds, and the anchor chain sites of CDs and SP were limited. Therefore, it was necessary to optimize the dosage of SP. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence increased with the addition of SP from 1.25 μM to 10 μM, but it did not increase after 10 μM, so it was considered that 10 μM was the optimal concentration of SP for labeling CDs.

Moreover, the use of SP@CDs alone could produce fluorescence, and the presence of SP@CDs competing with SP@CDs/CoOOH for binding sites could lead to a decrease in signal amplification ability when preparing SP@CDs/CoOOH. Therefore, the ratio of CDs to CoOOH was optimized. Simply, the ratio of CDs to CoOOH was set as 1:5, 1:2, 1:1, 5:1, and 10:1 respectively. As shown in Fig. 4C, the strongest fluorescence was observed as the ratio increased to 5:1. However, the fluorescence did not continue to increase as the ratio increased to 10:1. Thus, the optimal ratio of CDs to CoOOH was considered as 5:1.

Then, the fluorescence of CDs in SP@CDs/CoOOH was recovered by adding AA to eliminate the quenching effect of CoOOH. Therefore, it was essential to explore the optimal concentration of AA. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence increased with the increasing concentration of AA in the range from 0.1 μM to 2.0 μM, while decreased as the concentration of AA further increased from 2.0 μM to 3.0 μM, which could be attributed that excessive AA might quench CDs fluorescence. In detail, because CDs had many amino functional groups on the surface, which made them vulnerable to the influence of environmental pH. The change of pH might cause the change of n-π* electron transition of C=O bond of CDs and the change of π-π* electron transition of conjugate C=C bond of CDs, thus affecting the fluorescence properties of CDs [33,34]. As a result, 2.0 μM was considered as the optimal concentration of AA.

Furthermore, the first signal amplification strategy was that PML/RARα could combine with DNAzyme A and DNAzyme B into a complete DNAzyme, which could shear HP and generate trigger, and appropriate incubation time for DNAzyme and HP was crucial to the signal amplification. As shown in Fig. 4E, the fluorescence increased with the extension of incubation time from 30 min to 120 min, while the fluorescence did not increase after 120 min. In general, the signal amplification ability would be weakened if the incubation time was short, while the fluorescence would not increase even though the incubation time was prolonged for the reaction would reach equilibrium. Therefore, 120 min was adopted as the optimal incubation time for DNAzyme and HP.

Finally, the second signal amplification strategy was that trigger could hybridize with SP and CP simultaneously, and after fixing SP@CDs/CoOOH on the microplate, AA could reduce CoOOH to release multiple CDs. Thus, suitable incubation time was of great significance for the anchor chain of fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 5 F, the fluorescence was significantly enhanced with the extension of incubation time in the range from 15 min to 45 min, however, the fluorescence did not increase significantly as it entered the plateau phase after 45 min. Therefore, 45 min was adopted as the optimal incubation time for trigger and SP@CDs/CoOOH.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, different concentrations of PML/RARα could lead to different detection performance, and after double signal amplification, the difference would be presented through the fluorescence signal output of CDs, which was related to the concentration of PML/RARα. As shown in Fig. 5A, with the increase of PML/RARα concentration, the fluorescence signal enhanced. Besides, in the range from 102 fM to 107 fM, there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence signal and PML/RARα concentration. Moreover, the linear regression equation was Y = 447.2 ✕ lg C - 304, the correlation coefficient R2 was 0.9987, and the detection limit was 15.7 fM (Fig. 5B). Compared with other biosensors for PML/RARα fusion gene detection, wider linear range or lower detection limit were reached in this developed biosensor (Table S2), which might be mainly due to the superior amplification signal ability of DNAzyme combining with CDs/CoOOH. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 5C, non-complementary sequence (NC) (column a), the normal PML gene (column b) or RARα gene (column c) caused almost similar signal approximating to blank control, and the PML/RARα (S) caused minor signal increase (column d). As expected, because PML/RARα could be analyzed specifically by the developed biosensor, there was a remarkable fluorescence signal increasing shown in column e. The results indicated that only the presence of target PML/RARα could activate cascade amplification to generate a distinct fluorescence signal, and also confirmed that the biosensing strategy had good specificity. These results revealed that different interfering nucleic acid sequences had little influence on the specificity of the developed biosensor for PML/RARα detection, manifesting the high specificity of the developed biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of the developed biosensor for PML/RARα fuse gene detection in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practicality of the developed biosensor in complicated biological environment, we tested different concentrations of target PML/RARα in healthy human serum containing various plasma proteins, enzymes and other interference. The results in Fig. 6 A showed that with the increase of PML/RARα in the mixture samples, the fluorescence signal increased as well. In addition, as shown in Fig. 6B, the linear equation (Y = 378.9 × lg C – 572.6) with the linear correlation coefficient (R2) as 0.999, agreeing with results obtained in 3.6 Additionally, the limit of detection of this developed biosensor for PML/RARα in serum was as low as 223.82 fM, showing great potential for sensitive and accurate determination of PML/RARα in complicated biological environment.

A novel signal amplification detection system combining DNAzyme and CDs/CoOOH was designed to realize the rapid and sensitive detection of PML/RARα. In general, the specific recognition and signal amplification based on DNAzyme and the fluorescence signal amplification strategy based on CDs/CoOOH were carried out in homogeneous phase, ensuring an effective recognition reaction. Moreover, it was worth noting that CoOOH could significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs before the addition of AA to obtain a lower background signal. Then, the fluorescence recovery of CDs was induced by the addition of AA to discompose CoOOH, and the strong signal output was obtained. More importantly, through the combination of DNAzyme and CDs/CoOOH, the designed signal mode could not only significantly amplify the fluorescence signal output, but also make the enzyme activity less susceptible to various factors in the reaction system compared with enzyme-depended methods. However, clinical performance verification has not been completed in this study. Overall, the developed strategy showed significant specificity and sensitivity in detecting PML/RARα with a detection limit of 15.7 fM, showing potential clinical application value in the diagnosis and treatment of APL.

Chenlan Shen: Software, Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Liang Zhong: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization, Resources, Software. Peng Wan: Resources, Validation, Software. Hengke Jia: Data curation, Conceptualization. Beizhong Liu: Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The following funds supported this study: the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81772280), the Project of Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau (No. cstc2022ycjh-bgzxm0034), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2023NSFSC1481), the Postdoctoral Research Foundation of West China Hospital of Sichuan University (2023HXBH122) and the Sichuan University postdoctoral interdisciplinary Innovation Fund (10822041A2111).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.341623.
